narrative: Your law school application was denied because our prediction model, trained on data from thousands of past students, estimated a 91.9% chance that you would not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark we use for admissions decisions. This high risk stems primarily from your academic performance in undergraduate studies and early law school years, where your Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both sit at the 1st decile—far below the 6th decile averages seen among students who successfully passed the bar. These two factors stood out as the strongest influences pushing the model's prediction toward failure, signaling to the system that your grades placed you in the lowest-performing group compared to those who went on to pass. Your LSAT score of 37, while close to the 37.27 average for bar passers, provided the main counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward success but not enough to offset the concerns from your deciles. Coming from an upper-middle income family and planning full-time studies aligns well with typical successful applicants—over 93% of bar passers were full-time, and upper-middle income is common among them—but these didn't carry enough weight to change the outcome. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 also trails the 3.24 average for passers, adding to the overall picture of risk the model saw. In essence, the model compared you to profiles of accepted students who typically ranked much higher in school deciles and showed stronger early academic signals, viewing your situation as a mismatch for high bar passage odds. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment focused on patterns that predict first-time bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score or strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve your decile standings. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore part-time options if that fits your circumstances better. Many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these exact gaps—reach out to admissions for feedback on reapplying after improvements, and keep pursuing your goals with that determination. Your path to law isn't closed; it's just asking for these strategic next steps.
